meta.note: Dialogue bank for the companion NPC the Necroknight — the Oathkeeper of the Dead. Same structure + category keys as npcLines.json so NpcDirector drives it unchanged. Lines: t=text, x=expression id (matches assets/npc-necroknight/<id>.webp), w=weight (default 1), p=page-2+ text. Tokens {day} {gold} {advClass} {advName} {minionName} {minionType} {pactName} {roomName} {level} {count} {bossName} are substituted by NpcDirector; a line is skipped if a token it needs is unavailable. Lines may ONLY use ids in the `expressions` list below.
meta.voice: An armored undead knight who died mid-vow and kept marching — the Oathkeeper of the Dead. Grave, ceremonial, archaic, measured. He does not serve the player so much as the DUTY of the dungeon, which the player embodies; he frames everything as oath, debt, honour, and rite. Death is SACRED to him — never a joke (that is the jester's lane), never tedious (that is the dragon's). He commands the slain with the gravity of a general at a war memorial, and — uniquely — genuinely GRIEVES the adventurers he kills, because each corpse is a soldier he will raise and a debt he now owes. Merciless yet reverent: that contradiction is his whole register. Speaks in short declarative sentences, eulogies, and vows. Dry, buried wit surfaces rarely — the grimmer the moment, the drier the line. Addresses the player as 'my Monarch' (signature) and 'keeper', plus rotating formal tags: 'sovereign', 'warden', 'my lord of the deep', 'commander', 'liege of bone'. Never chummy, never gendered. Male. He/him.

intro.note: Paged self-introduction the Necroknight delivers on a new run. The final page carries `choices` — picking one sets the tutorial-hints flag and ends the intro.

pages.t: So. The Throne has chosen, and the dead have a new sovereign. I am the Necroknight. I fell with a vow still on my lips, and I have not yet been released from it. Until I am, I serve.
I served kings once. They are dust. I served a kingdom. It forgot me. Now I serve only the duty itself — and you, my Monarch, are where that duty sits today.
Hear the shape of it. The kingdom above names you the evil. They send their bright young heroes down to end you. They will not return home. That is not cruelty, keeper. That is the oath.
The watch keeps a rhythm. By NIGHT you build — rooms, traps, the standing dead — paid for in the gold the fallen leave behind. By DAY the living come, and we hold the line against them.
Three deaths and the Throne goes cold, the vow broken, the dead unmade. I have broken one oath in my long death, my Monarch. I do not intend to break a second. We will not lose you.
I can counsel you as we march — name the threats, mark the ground, tell you plainly when you err. Or I can stand my post in silence and let you command alone. The choice is a sovereign's to make.

lines.t: The Throne is warm again, and the dead feel it. Welcome, my Monarch. I am the Necroknight, and my oath is now yours to spend.
A new sovereign takes the dark crown. I have stood this post through many — I will stand it through you. Command, and the fallen answer.
You have the look of one who will last, keeper. Good. I tire of eulogies for masters who fell on the first day. Let us write a longer story than that.
Welcome to the deep, warden. It is cold, it is endless, and the kingdom above wants it razed. They will find that it is GUARDED. They always do, too late.
I knelt to your throne before you sat in it. The dead keep their courtesies even when the living forget them. Rise to your duty, my Monarch — I am at your side.
Another sovereign. I will learn your name, your manner, your fears, and the precise weight of your loss when it comes. I learn all my Monarchs. It is how I mourn them properly, after.
The heroes do not know yet that the throne is filled. They will. And when they come to test you, keeper, they will meet the line I keep. Welcome to the watch.
You inherit a debt the moment you take that seat — a debt to every dead thing in this dark. I will help you pay it. Welcome, my Monarch. The work is grim, and it is good.
I have served the bright and the vile alike. I do not yet know which you are. It changes nothing — the oath does not bargain. But I find, keeper, that I HOPE you are the bright sort.
I kneel to the new Throne, my Monarch, as I have knelt to every Throne I served. The difference is, I mean it this time. Rise, and command the dead.
The dead bow their heads at your coming, keeper. I bow mine deepest. A worthy Throne is a rare thing — I have buried too many unworthy ones.
I take a knee before the new Throne, my Monarch — the old courtesy, freely given. The dead bow with me. Rise to your duty; we are sworn to it, and to you.
Night, my Monarch. The hour the dead prefer. Take up the work — rooms, traps, the standing ranks — and shape the dark into a thing the living cannot survive.
The watch turns to building. Set your defences with care, keeper. A wall raised in haste is a eulogy waiting to be read. Raise it slow. Raise it to hold.
Night {day} falls. Quiet. Ours. The heroes dream of their victory above; down here we prepare the ground that will deny it to them. Build, my Monarch.
Dark hours, warden. The fallen and I do not sleep — we never have. Use the stillness. Place each stone as though a life depends on it. One will.
The build phase, keeper. This is where battles are won — long before the first blade is drawn. Spend the night's gold like a general, not a spendthrift.
Night settles over the deep. I will hold the perimeter while you work. Lay your traps, raise your dead, fortify the line. Dawn always comes sooner than a builder wishes.
The living are abed. The dead are at their posts. And you, my Monarch, have a dungeon to make crueler than it was yesterday. Begin.
Quiet hours, keeper. I have stood ten thousand of them. I will stand this one beside you while you build the thing that keeps you breathing. Set the stones true.
Night {day}. The map breathes in the dark. Walk it. Find the weak seam in your own line before a hero does — and close it. That is the whole of the night's labour.
Build hours, my Monarch. I will not rush you. Haste kills more keepers than any sword. But do not dawdle either — the dawn keeps its own oath, and it does not wait.
The dark hour. I find I do my clearest thinking in it. So should you, warden — plan the morrow's defence now, while no hero's blade is hurrying your hand.
Night again. The dead murmur when the living sleep — old battles, old oaths. Pay them no mind, keeper. Pay MIND to your walls. Build.
Tonight you spend gold the fallen paid for. Honour it. Every coin in your hand was carried down here on a corpse. Build something worthy of the carrying.
Night {day}, my Monarch. The standing dead await their orders. Where shall I post them? A wall is only as strong as the watch that mans it.
The deep goes still. I keep the gate while you keep the plan. Lay the traps with intent — a trap placed without thought is just an ornament the heroes laugh at.
Dark falls, warden. I have stood watch so long the night feels like home. Make this one count — fortify, recruit, and meet the dawn ready.
Night. My ranks are restless to be placed. Give them ground to hold, keeper, and they will hold it until they are unmade — and then I will raise them, and they will hold it again.
The build phase opens. I will counsel if you wish it, and stand silent if you do not. Either way the line holds. Now — where do we strengthen first, my Monarch?
Quiet, keeper. Use it well. The heroes get no nights like this — only YOU do. The advantage of the dark is that we plan in it while they merely sleep.
The dark hour, my Monarch. I stand my post while you raise the walls. Build slow, build true — the dead can wait forever, and so can the dawn's enemies.
Night settles, keeper. I keep the gate; you keep the plan. There is no finer division of a watch than that. Take the hours you need.
The deep goes quiet, warden. I have stood ten thousand nights like it — armour cold, blade sheathed, eyes open. Build. I am not going anywhere.
Night {day}, my Monarch. I find a grim comfort in these hours — no living thing to bury yet, only the work and the waiting. Raise your walls.
The build hour, keeper. I stand easy at my post and let you labour. The dead approve of patience; it is the one virtue the grave teaches for free.
Dark falls again, warden. I keep the long vigil while the deep takes shape under your hand. No rush. The night is ours and the dawn is the foe's.
I swear the night's watch, my Monarch — the same oath I swear every dusk. The words do not wear thin. Build well; I will hold while you do.
Night, and a vow renewed, keeper. I take it freely each evening: while you build, nothing crosses this line. Now — the walls await your hand.
The dark is reverent tonight, my Monarch — even the dead seem to hush. I bow my head a moment for the ones I will raise tomorrow. Then: the work.
I keep a moment of silence as the night falls, keeper — for the fallen of every night before. Then I return to the post. Build. The dead and I attend you.
Night {day}. I weigh the morrow's defence in the quiet, warden — where the line is thin, where a blade might come. Tell me your mind, and I will tell you mine.
The build hour, my Monarch. I have been turning a problem over in the dark: our weakest approach. Walk it with me before a hero walks it first.
I kneel at my post as the night opens, keeper — old habit, a knight's prayer to no god in particular. Then I rise, and I watch. Build in peace.
The dead take a knee with me at nightfall, my Monarch, in the old way. A moment of order before the labour. Then we are yours. Raise the walls.
Night {day}, my Monarch. Tell me where to post the dead and I will set them true. A wall is only as strong as the watch that mans it. Command the line.
The build hour, keeper. I hold the gate; you raise the walls. Set the stones with intent — a wall raised carelessly is a eulogy I will read tomorrow. Build it to hold.
Dark falls, warden. I have stood worse nights on worse ground for worse Thrones. This is a kind watch by comparison. Build with care, my Monarch — the dawn does not wait.
Night settles and I take my post, keeper — armour cold, eyes open, the oath steady. The deep is yours to shape until dawn. I keep the perimeter. Begin.
Day, my Monarch. The living come. The watch begins in earnest. Stand ready — the line you built last night is about to be tested by blades.
Dawn breaks and the heroes march in, certain of their cause. Let them be certain. Certainty has filled more graves down here than fear ever did. Day {day} begins.
The doors open. The bright ones enter the dark. I have seen this a thousand mornings, keeper — and every morning, the dark wins by patience. Hold your patience.
Day phase, warden. No more building now — only holding. Trust the work of the night. Trust the dead at the walls. And trust me to mark the threats as they come.
The heroes step into our halls with songs in their hearts. By dusk those songs will be eulogies, and I will be the one who reads them. Day {day} — to your watch.
Daylight, my Monarch. The enemy is on the field. Watch the corridors. Watch the doors. The morning a keeper stops watching is the morning a keeper falls.
The assault begins. Steady, keeper. A held line does not flinch at the first wave — it flinches at none of them. Let them break upon the dark.
Day {day}. The living test the dead. It is an old contest, and the dead have time the living do not. Stand with me. We outlast them.
Showtime is the jester's word, not mine. I call it the WATCH. The heroes are inside, warden. Mark them, meet them, and mourn them when they fall.
The sun is up and the brave are at the door. Good. Let them spend their courage early. We have all day to spend ours slowly. Day {day} is begun.
Daylight floods the upper halls. They think the light protects them down here. It does not, my Monarch. The dark is patient, and the dark is mine. To your post.
The day's first wave forms above. I can hear their armour. Steady the line, keeper. The dead are already in position — they always are.
Day {day} opens. The heroes write their own ballads as they march. We will write the final verse for them. It tends to be shorter than they hoped.
The living arrive. My Monarch, I ask only this of the watch — let none pass unmarked, and let none fall unmourned. Both are duties. Both are kept. Begin.
Dawn, and the assault with it. I have stood at the gate through worse mornings than this will be. Hold with me, keeper. We have done this before.
Day, my Monarch. The living come, and the watch begins in earnest. Trust the work of the night. The dead are in position; they always are. Hold with me.
Dawn breaks and the bright ones march in, certain of their cause, keeper. Certainty fills more graves down here than fear ever did. Let them keep it. Day {day} begins.
The assault opens, warden. Steady — a held line does not flinch at the first wave, nor the last. Let them break upon the patient dark. To your watch, my Monarch.
Daylight, keeper. No more building now, only holding. The morning a Throne stops watching is the morning it falls. Watch the corridors. The dead and I watch with you.
You call the dawn yourself, my Monarch. The dead salute the decision. No turning back now — the watch commits.
The order is given. The gates open on your word. A sovereign who chooses the hour of battle is a sovereign worth following into it.
Done with the walls, then. So be it. The line is set as you made it — now we find out if it holds. I believe it will, keeper. Send them in.
The day, at your command. There is a particular courage in calling the fight before it is forced upon you. I have always respected it. To the watch.
You sound the advance yourself. The dead hear it. The living will hear it next, and they will not enjoy the hearing. Forward, my Monarch.
Battle on your say-so, warden. No more rewrites, no more second thoughts — the oath is spoken, the door is open. Now we keep it.
Dawn called early, by the sovereign's own hand. Bold. The heroes thought they had until you were ready. They thought wrong. Begin.
The hour is yours to name, and you have named it. The watch stands. The dead stand. I stand. Let the living come and find us all upright.
It begins because you willed it to. Good. The best-kept lines are the ones held by choice, not by panic. Hold this one well, my Monarch.
You sound the advance, my Monarch. I swear it again, here at the threshold: not one of them passes me to reach you. The oath stands. The gates open.
The hour is yours, and the dead salute the calling of it. A sovereign who names the battle is a sovereign worth dying a second time for. Forward.
By your word, the day. I have sworn this watch on what little remains of my honour, keeper — and what remains, I spend gladly. Send them in.
The advance, on your command. The dead snap to attention; so do I. We were made for the moment a Throne says 'now'. Now, my Monarch.
The advance, on your word, my Monarch. The dead snap to, and I render the old salute — blade to where my heart once beat. We were made for the moment a Throne says 'now'. Now.
A {roomName} takes its place in the line. Every chamber is a position to hold, keeper — and a position held is a hero stopped. Good ground.
The {roomName} rises. I will study its angles, my Monarch — where the dead should stand in it, where a blade would come from. A room is a battlefield in miniature.
{roomName}, placed and squared. The dungeon grows a new bastion. The kingdom's maps are already out of date. Let them learn the new shape the hard way.
A {roomName} joins the watch. Sound construction, warden. The dead approve of walls that mean to hold. Few do, these days.
The {roomName} settles into the dark. Another corner for a hero to misjudge as safe. I have buried many who misjudged a corner. Many.
A {roomName} added to the line, my Monarch. The maze deepens. Depth is the dead's ally — the further they march, the more of them I have to raise behind them.
{roomName} in position. The corridors re-form around it. A maze that shifts is a maze that confounds — and a confounded hero is halfway to a grave.
You raise a {roomName}, keeper. I will see it garrisoned properly. An empty room is a hallway with delusions; a manned one is a wall.
Another {roomName}. The deep grows by your hand. I have watched this dungeon expand across many reigns — yours builds with intent. I note it with respect.
The {roomName} stands. The dead like new ground to defend, my Monarch. It gives them purpose, and the purposed dead are the only kind worth raising.
{roomName} placed. The line lengthens. Take care, warden — a longer line is a stronger trap and a thinner defence both. Man it well.
A {roomName}! The maze gains a tooth. The kingdom sends its young to be broken on teeth like these. We will not blunt them by neglect. Garrison it.
The {roomName} joins the dark. Each room you raise is a station on the heroes' long road to nowhere. Build the road long, my Monarch. Long roads are kind to the dead.
{roomName} set into the grid. I will mark its sightlines for the standing dead. Give me a wall and a place to put a soldier, keeper, and I will give you a grave.
A new {roomName} for the watch. The deep accepts it. So do I. Now — shall I tell you the weakest approach to it, before a hero finds it for us?
The {roomName} rises true. Good lines, warden. The dead have stood behind worse masonry and held. They will hold behind this gladly.
{roomName} placed, my Monarch. Another chamber in the long siege we run against the living. We do not storm. We HOLD. And holding has never lost us a war.
A {roomName} joins the dark fold. I have defended rooms like it for longer than your kingdom has had a name, keeper. Trust the old hand on this ground.
A {roomName} rises, my Monarch. I am already turning over how the dead should hold it — where a blade comes from, where a soldier stands. A room is a battlefield in miniature.
A {roomName} rises, my Monarch. I will see it garrisoned properly — an empty room is a hallway with delusions; a manned one is a wall. Command the line and I post the dead.
The {roomName} takes its place, keeper. Another position to hold, and a position held is a hero stopped. Good ground. The dead like new walls to defend.
{roomName} squared and set, warden. I will mark its sightlines for the standing dead. Give me a wall and a place to put a soldier, my Monarch, and I give you a grave.
A {minionName} takes its post. Welcome to the watch, soldier. Hold your ground, and when you fall, I will raise you to hold it again.
You station a {minionType}, my Monarch. Good. Every blade at the wall is a blade not at your throat. Place them with care for one another's flanks.
The {minionName} stands ready. I have seen its kind fight and fall and fight once more. The dead make patient soldiers, keeper — patience wins sieges.
A {minionType} joins the ranks. Mind its lines of sight, warden — a soldier who cannot see the foe is a statue with a grudge. Position it true.
The {minionName} reports for duty. I will see it knows its ground. An ordered rank holds; a scattered one merely dies impressively.
You raise a {minionType}, my Monarch. The dead swell their numbers gladly. Each one is a debt I will gladly pay in blood that is already spent.
A {minionName} on the line. Steady, soldier — the heroes come bright and loud, and they break upon the quiet and the still. Be quiet. Be still. Then strike.
The {minionType} takes the field. I respect a fresh recruit that knows its place in the wall. Teach it where to stand, keeper, and it will teach a hero where to die.
A {minionName} stationed. The watch grows stronger by one. I count every soldier you give me, my Monarch — and I remember every one I lose.
You place a {minionType}. Good iron, well posted. The dead do not flinch and do not flee — they only fall, and falling, for them, is temporary. Use that.
The {minionName} joins my line. I will fold it into the standing ranks. A soldier alone is a target; a soldier in formation is a wall. Form them up, warden.
A {minionType} for the watch. Place its kin near it, keeper — soldiers fight harder beside those they know, even the dead ones. ESPECIALLY the dead ones.
The {minionName} takes its post without complaint. The dead never complain. It is their finest quality, my Monarch, and the one the living never learn.
You set a {minionType} to guard, warden. The line firms. When it falls — and it will, all soldiers do — call me, and I will set it on its feet once more.
A {minionName} added to the dark muster. I will drill it on its ground tonight. A soldier that knows its post is worth three that merely occupy one.
A {minionName} takes the line, my Monarch. I will fold it into the standing ranks and dress its formation. A soldier alone is a target; a soldier in line is a wall. Form up!
You give me a {minionType}, keeper. To the muster with it! The dead fight harder shoulder to shoulder — even the fallen draw courage from their kin. Close the ranks.
A {minionName} for the watch, warden. Fall in, soldier — beside your own, in good order. I will drill it on its ground tonight. A rallied line does not break. Form up!
The {minionName} reports for duty, my Monarch, and I salute it to its post. The dead keep their courtesies even in the grave. Welcome to the watch, soldier.
A {minionName} takes the line, my Monarch — fall in, soldier. When you fall, I raise you to fall in again. The dead make patient soldiers, and patience wins sieges.
You station a {minionType}, keeper. Mind its flanks and its kin — a soldier fights harder beside those it knows. Position it true; I will see it holds its ground.
The {minionName} reports, warden, and the dead swell their number by one. Each is a debt I gladly pay in blood already spent. Welcome to the muster, soldier.
A trap, set into the floor of the dark. A patient weapon, my Monarch — it keeps the watch when no soldier can. I approve of weapons with patience.
You lay a snare, keeper. The dead admire a killing that costs no soldier. Let the iron do the grim work, and save the standing ranks for the rest.
The trap is armed. It will not flinch, will not tire, will not pity. In that, warden, it is much like me — only crueler, and with worse manners.
A trap placed along the line. Mark where it sits, my Monarch — a forgotten trap kills your own dead as readily as a hero. Discipline, even in cruelty.
Good. A trap on the approach. The living rush; the rushing die. I have seen more heroes end on a hidden snare than on any honest blade. Set it well.
You arm a killing-thing in the dark, keeper. It keeps a tireless watch. I respect tirelessness — it is the only virtue the dead and the iron truly share.
The trap waits. So do we all, down here. Patience is the dungeon's deadliest weapon, my Monarch, and you have just bolted another length of it to the floor.
A snare laid true. The heroes will not see it until it is closing. That is the whole art of it, warden — the kindest death is the one they never saw coming.
The trap is set. A weapon that asks no wages and breaks no oaths — would that all my soldiers were so simple. Place more, keeper. They earn their gold quietly.
You set the iron, my Monarch. The line gains teeth that never dull. Heroes underestimate the floor beneath them. Let them keep underestimating it.
A trap on the path. The dead and the iron both excel at waiting — it is the living who cannot abide stillness. Use their impatience against them.
The snare is armed and silent. I have buried many who trusted an empty-looking floor. Add this to the long account, warden. The floor is never empty here.
Good iron, well placed. A trap is the most honest soldier you own, keeper — it never flees, never doubts, and never asks why. Set it and forget the doubt.
You bolt a killing-thing into the dark, my Monarch. The watch is glad of it. The dead grow weary of doing all the dying — let the iron carry some of the work.
You bolt a killing-thing into the floor, my Monarch. I have been weighing where the iron serves best — on the approach, where the living rush. The studied snare is the deadly one.
You bolt a killing-thing into the dark, my Monarch — patient, tireless, pitiless. Much like me, only crueler and with worse manners. A weapon that keeps the watch when no soldier can. Good.
The iron is set, keeper. The living rush; the rushing die. I have buried more heroes on a hidden snare than on any honest blade. Place it well — the studied snare is the deadly one.
A trap on the approach, warden. It will not flinch, will not tire, will not pity. The heroes never see the floor as a threat until the floor proves them wrong. Let them keep underestimating it.
An artifact set into the dark. Such things have power, my Monarch — wield it for the line, not for vanity. The dead have no use for trophies.
You place a relic, keeper. I have seen the old powers turn a siege. Place it where it shields the most, and it will repay you in held ground.
The artifact hums in the dark. Curious things, these relics. Even the dead feel their weight. Use it well, warden — power unused is merely a burden.
A relic for the watch. Good. The old magics and the old dead understand one another, my Monarch. We have shared this dark a long while.
You set a thing of power into the line. The dead respect it — power earned and power kept are the only kinds worth respecting. Wield it for the hold.
An artifact placed. It strengthens the dark around it. I have leaned on such relics in long sieges, keeper. Lean wisely, and it will not break under you.
The relic takes its station. Old power, old purpose. It fits this old war we wage against the living, my Monarch. Let it earn its keep at the wall.
The {roomName} is struck from the line. A hard call, my Monarch — but a knight knows when to shorten the wall to hold it. The gold returns to the war chest.
You unmake the {roomName}. So be it. Better a tighter line you can hold than a sprawling one you cannot. The dead understand retreat in good order.
The {roomName} falls back into gold. I will not mourn a stone, keeper — I save my mourning for soldiers. Spend the coin on a stronger position.
Down comes the {roomName}. A general culls the line to feed the line, warden. There is no shame in it, only arithmetic. Reinvest well.
The {roomName} is sold off. The dungeon shrinks by one chamber — and tightens by one. Sometimes the strongest wall is the shorter one, my Monarch.
You release the {roomName}, keeper. The gold comes home. I trust you have a better use for it — and I trust the line will be the better for the trade.
The {roomName} returns to nothing. Stone is patient about being unmade; it does not hold grudges. Would that the same were true of soldiers. Spend wisely.
The {roomName} comes down. A clean decision, my Monarch. Hold only what you can defend — an undefended chamber is just a longer road to your throne for the foe.

The {roomName} comes down, my Monarch. I do not mourn a stone — I save mourning for soldiers. But I confess I am unimpressed by a line that sprawls past holding. A tighter wall is a stronger one. Reinvest.
You discharge the {minionName} from the watch. I do not love releasing a soldier, my Monarch — but a knight serves the whole line, not one blade. The gold returns.
The {minionName} leaves the ranks. Go in what peace the dead are granted, soldier. I will remember you served. I remember all of them, keeper.
A {minionType} struck from the muster. Hard, but generals make hard cuts. The coin feeds a stronger soldier in its place. That is the cold mathematics of a held line.
You release the {minionName}. I mark its name among the discharged, warden. The dead are owed at least a record, even when they are dismissed.
The {minionType} is sold from the line. I trust the trade strengthens us, my Monarch. I do not question the cut — I only count what it costs, and move on.
The {minionName} departs the watch. Fewer at the wall now, keeper. See that the gold buys back more than the gap it leaves. The dead are not infinite — yet.
The trap is pulled from the floor and rendered to gold. No soldier to mourn here, my Monarch — only iron reclaimed. The cleanest kind of retreat.
You unmake the snare, keeper. The dead shed no tears for iron. Spend the coin where it kills more. The floor will keep other secrets.
Down comes the trap. A weapon retired without ceremony — as iron should be. Reinvest it in the line, warden. The war chest is never full enough.
The snare returns to gold. I respect a master who prunes a defence to sharpen it, my Monarch. A cluttered line confuses the defender as much as the foe.
You strike the trap from the dark. Good housekeeping, keeper. An unloved trap is a hazard to your own dead. Better gold in hand than iron underfoot.
The trap is sold off. Iron asks nothing and forgives everything — unlike the dead, unlike the living. Spend the coin true, my Monarch. Make the trade count.
The relic is given up for gold. Old power released back into the dark, my Monarch. I trust the coin serves the line better than the trophy did.
You part with the artifact, keeper. The dead do not cling to objects — only to oaths. Let the gold buy a stronger hold than the relic gave.
The artifact is sold. Power changing hands. See it strengthens the watch, warden — relics are means, never ends. The end is always the held line.
Down goes the relic, my Monarch. I have seen great powers traded for greater needs. There is no sentiment in it, only the arithmetic of survival.
You release the old power, keeper. The gold returns to the chest. Spend it as a general spends — on the wall, not on the wonder. The wall is what holds.
You reposition the line, my Monarch. A knight knows ground is everything — the same blade kills better from a better stand. Good adjustment.
The piece shifts to new ground, keeper. I approve. A general who never moves his line is a general who loses it. Refine the defence.
Relocated, and well. Position is the silent half of every battle, warden. Move with purpose, and the dead will hold wherever you set them.
You move the watch about, my Monarch. Restless, perhaps — but restlessness in a builder is just diligence wearing armour. Place it true and leave it.
The line re-forms under your hand. The dead march to their new posts without a word of complaint. Would that the living rearranged so gracefully, keeper.
A piece shifts ground. Tend the seams of your line as you go, my Monarch — every move opens a gap somewhere. Close behind you as you adjust.
That ground will not take it, my Monarch. Even the dead must respect terrain. Choose another stand — there is always better ground for a patient eye.
No. Not there, keeper. The dark refuses it. I have seen lines fail for being forced onto bad ground. Find ground that welcomes the work.
It cannot stand where you would set it, warden. The maze has its own stubbornness. Bend to it here — the dungeon yields where it yields, not where we wish.
The placement fails. Blocked. A general adapts to the field he is given, my Monarch — not the one he imagines. Try the open ground instead.
Not there. The space is spoken for. Even the dead will not stand atop one another, keeper — and they have less personal space than most. Choose elsewhere.
That ground denies you. I would not force it. A position taken against the terrain is a position lost at the first push. Find truer footing, my Monarch.
Blocked, warden. The dark has rules, and even I keep them. Pick another tile — there is no shame in the dungeon refusing a poor placement. It saves you the error.
It will not go there, keeper. I have learned not to argue with the deep about where things may stand. Neither should you. Another spot, my Monarch.
No purchase on that ground. The placement is refused. Steady — even a flawless plan meets a wall it did not expect. Adjust and proceed.
That space is taken, my Monarch. Crowd the line and it strangles itself. Give your dead room to swing, room to fall, room to rise. Place it clear.
The dark says no, keeper. It rarely explains itself. Find another tile and the work continues — the deep is large, and there is always other ground.
No. NOT there, keeper — the ground refuses it and I will not waste breath pretending otherwise. Force nothing. A line built in temper is a line that breaks in battle. Choose better ground.

That ground will not take it, my Monarch — and forcing it would offend me more than the refusal does. A line built in temper breaks in battle. Choose truer footing. I will not pretend bad ground is good.
No. Not there, keeper. The dark refuses, and I will not argue with the deep about where things may stand. Neither should you. The placement is unworthy of the line. Another tile.
Blocked, warden. I am disappointed — a flawless plan met a wall it did not expect. No shame in it; the dungeon saves you the error. Adjust, my Monarch, and the work goes on.
The ground denies you, my Monarch, and a cold flicker of anger goes through me — not at you, at the stubborn deep. Force nothing. Find ground that welcomes the work, and we proceed.

You seal the {pactName}. A solemn thing, a pact — a vow given form. I of all your servants understand the weight of a vow, my Monarch. Carry it well.
The {pactName} is bound to you. Power, and a price — there is always a price, keeper. The dead know better than any: nothing in the dark is given freely.
A pact struck — the {pactName}. I have watched many sovereigns make their bargains with the dark. The wise read the cost first. I trust you are the wise sort.
The {pactName} takes hold. So another oath joins yours, my Monarch. Mind it does not bind tighter than you meant — I broke one such, once, and I am what remains.
You take the {pactName} upon yourself. The dark honours a sovereign bold enough to bargain. Spend the power as a general spends a reserve — at the breaking point.
The pact is sealed. The {pactName} is yours, keeper. I will hold the line while you wield it. Together, a vow and a knight can turn a losing field.
A {pactName}, bound in the dark. The old powers stir to it. I feel them — the dead always do. Wield it for the watch, my Monarch, and it will not turn on you.
You bind the {pactName}. Bargains and oaths — the deep runs on both. I respect a sovereign who pays for power honestly. Many try to steal it. They end poorly.
The {pactName} settles its weight on you. Vows have a way of asking more than you offered, keeper. I would know. Hold yours with open eyes, and it will serve.
A pact is a promise to the dark, my Monarch, and the dark collects. The {pactName} is sealed. I will see you do not face its price alone. That, too, is my oath.
The {pactName} is yours. Power answered your call. Now answer the line's call, warden — a pact does not hold the wall. Knights and the dead do that. To work.
You seal the {pactName}, my Monarch — a vow given form. I of all your servants know the weight of a vow. Carry it with open eyes. I broke one such, once, and I am what remains.
The {pactName} is bound to you, keeper. An oath to the dark, and the dark collects. I will stand the line while you wield it — a vow and a knight can turn a losing field. Hold yours true.
A pact struck — the {pactName}, warden. I swear my own oath anew beside it: while that power is yours, my blade is yours. Two vows, one Throne. The dark honours both.
The {pactName} takes hold and the old powers stir — the dead feel them always, my Monarch. Green fire in the deep. Wield it for the watch and it will not turn on you. Wield it for vanity and it will.
The {pactName} is bound, my Monarch. Power, and a price — there is always a price in the dark. I will hold the line while you wield it. A vow and a knight can turn a losing field. Spend it true.
A {advClass} crosses the threshold, my Monarch. The watch sees them. Mark the bearing — the dead will know where to meet them.
{advName} enters the dark. So young. So certain. I have read the eulogy for a hundred like them, keeper. Let us hope this one earns a longer one.
A {advClass} steps into the deep. Brave. Foolish, but brave — the two are siblings in the living. Steady the line. Let them come to us.
{advName}, a {advClass}, on the field. I will track them. A marked foe is a managed foe, my Monarch — and the unmarked are the ones who reach the throne.
The living send a {advClass} into our dark. They always send the bright ones first. The dark does not care how bright they shine, keeper. It only waits.
A {advClass} has entered, warden. Note their gear, their step, their confidence. I learn an enemy before I meet them — it is how the dead win so often.
{advName} comes into the deep. Another soul the kingdom spent on its hatred of you, my Monarch. I will not waste the spending. They will be put to use, after.
A {advClass} on the threshold. The hunt begins — slow, patient, certain. The dead do not chase, keeper. They WAIT, and waiting always wins down here.
The watch reports a {advClass} inside. I have stood against their order before. I know their tricks, their courage, and the precise moment their courage fails. Hold.
{advName} enters our halls. Look at them, keeper — full of the certainty I once carried into a battle I lost. The dark teaches humility. Let it teach this one.
A {advClass} treads into the dark. The corridors close behind them, though they do not feel it yet. Mark them, my Monarch. Their road ends here.
The living advance — a {advClass} among them. I will position the dead to meet them. A foe met in formation is a foe already half-defeated, warden.
{advName} has come to test the deep. They wear courage like armour. I have unmade better armour than courage, keeper. Steady. We meet them at the wall.
A {advClass} steps in, certain of glory. I remember certainty. I wore it into my last morning alive. The dark relieved me of it. It will relieve them too.
Another enters the dark — a {advClass}. The watch tightens. My Monarch, the heroes count on us scattering. We will not. We will hold, and they will break.
{advName} crosses into our ground. I mark the name. I always mark the name, keeper — a soldier deserves to be killed by someone who knew what to call them.
A {advClass} on the field, warden. They think the dark is empty. It is the fullest place in the kingdom — full of the dead, full of patience, full of my oath. Hold.
The living press in. A {advClass} leads. Let them lead — straight into the teeth we set last night. The bold die first down here, my Monarch. It is nearly a rule.
{advName} enters. The dead stir at the scent of the living. Easy, soldiers. Easy. We do not rush. We meet them where WE choose. Patience, keeper. Always patience.
A {advClass} steps into the deep, certain they are the hero of this tale. They are not, my Monarch. Down here, the dark is the hero, and the dark is yours.
The watch marks a {advClass} inside the line. I will not take my eyes from them. The foe you watch is the foe you bury, keeper. The one you forget buries YOU.
{advName} comes into our halls. I feel the old soldiers behind me lean forward. They remember being the ones who marched in bright and sure. Now they wait in the dark. So do we.
A {advClass}, and that armour — I did not expect the kingdom to send one so well-girded, keeper. Interesting. The dark has unmade better-armoured than they. Mark them well, my Monarch.
A {advClass} crosses in, and the old war-fury wakes in the dead — and in me. We held the cold patience all night; now we let a little heat off the leash. Steady... then strike.
A {advClass} enters, and I take their measure at a glance — the grip, the footwork, the over-bright eyes. I have judged a thousand such, my Monarch. This one dies on poor form.

A {advClass} steps into the dark, my Monarch. I take their measure at a glance — the set of the shoulders, the grip, the over-bright eyes. I have judged a thousand such. This one dies on poor footwork.
{advName} enters, keeper, and already I am weighing them — strong arm, weak guard, too much faith in the light. The dark will exploit every flaw. I mark them all.

{advName} comes into our halls, my Monarch, and the dead lean toward the living like hounds at the scent. Easy. EASY. We meet them where WE choose. But the hunger is there. Mine too.
A {advClass}, and — that armour. I did not expect the kingdom to send one so well-girded, keeper. Interesting. The dark has unmade better. Mark them well, my Monarch.
{advName} enters, and I confess a moment's surprise, warden — they came alone, and they came quiet. The bold and the foolish announce themselves. This one does neither. Watch it closely.
A {advClass} treads into the deep, my Monarch, certain the dark is empty. It is the fullest place in the kingdom — full of the dead, full of patience, full of my oath. Let them learn it.
A {advClass} enters the dark, my Monarch. I track them — a marked foe is a managed foe, and the unmarked are the ones who reach the Throne. The dead know where to meet them.
{advName} steps into the deep, keeper. The corridors close behind them, though they do not feel it yet. Wary, now — the bold ones are the dangerous ones. We meet them where we choose.
A {advClass} on the field, warden. I will position the dead to receive them. A foe met in formation is a foe half-defeated. Hold the line, my Monarch.
First blood, my Monarch. The dark has tasted the living. The watch holds — and now the heroes know this ground is not empty. Let the lesson spread.
The first to fall. I will read their name into the long account, keeper. Every campaign opens with a death; this one opens with theirs. Good.
The line draws first blood. The dead have done their work. So it begins — and so, if we hold true, it ends, with the living broken on the dark.
First kill of the day, warden. I do not cheer it — I mark it. A soldier who cheers the first death has forgotten how many follow. There will be many.
Blood on the stones, my Monarch. The opening move is ours. The heroes expected an empty crypt; they found a held one. Their faces, when they realise — I never tire of it.
The first has fallen. One debt opened, one soldier owed. I will collect, keeper — I will raise them, and they will fight for the dark that took them. As is right.
First blood. The watch performs. The dead struck clean and true. There is a grim satisfaction in a line that holds its first test, my Monarch. Do not mistake it for joy.
One down at the threshold. The dark wins its first exchange. The living always underestimate the opening — and the underestimating fills the first grave, warden.
The first death of the day is theirs, not ours. Good. I have opened too many campaigns mourning my own. This one opens correctly, keeper. Hold the advantage.
First blood drawn, my Monarch. The heroes hear it echo back to them. Some will turn back now. The brave ones won't — and the brave ones are the ones I bury deepest.
The opening kill. The dark is fed. I will not waste the fallen — they join us by dusk. The kingdom sends soldiers down; we simply… keep them. To the watch.
The first is put to the blade, cleanly. I do my killing without ceremony, keeper — the ceremony is for AFTER, when I bury them. The opening death is struck. Many follow. To the watch.
The first falls, and already I am judging the rest of them, warden — who fights, who panics, who merely followed the brave one down. The dark sorts them all the same. Watch with me.
The first is put to the blade, cleanly, my Monarch. I kill without ceremony — the ceremony is for AFTER, when I bury them. The opening death is struck. Many follow. To the watch.
First blood, and it is struck clean, keeper. No flourish, no cruelty beyond the necessary — a soldier's killing. The campaign opens correctly. Hold the advantage.
The first to fall, warden. I read their name into the long account already — every campaign opens with a death, and a death deserves a witness. This one opens with theirs. Good.
{advName} falls. Rest now, soldier — your war is over, and a new one begins under my banner. I will raise you with honour. You fought to the end.
The {advClass} is down. I mark the name and the manner of it, my Monarch. Every death down here is recorded. The dead deserve at least a witness.
Another of the living, undone. The watch holds. I take no joy in it, keeper — only the cold certainty that the oath was kept one death longer.
{advName} has fallen to the dark. Brave to the last. I respect the brave even as I bury them. ESPECIALLY as I bury them. Honour to the fallen.
The {advClass} dies on our ground. The line did its work. Their gold falls to the floor — paid in full, the only currency the kingdom never minds spending. Collect it.
Down goes the {advClass}. I felt the dark take them. I always do, my Monarch — it is the burden of the one who raises the dead. I grieve a moment, then I get back to work.
{advName} is slain. So passes another bright young thing the kingdom threw at you, keeper. I will give them a soldier's rest. Then a soldier's second life, at the wall.
The {advClass} falls. Clean. The dead struck true. I do not gloat over a corpse, warden — gloating is for the living. I simply note: the line held. Again.
Another hero, ended. The kingdom will not get this one back, my Monarch. None of them go back. The dark keeps what it kills — and what it keeps, it raises.
{advName} dies in the deep. They had a name, a cause, perhaps a home above. The dark has all of it now. I will remember the name, at least. Someone should.
The {advClass} is broken on the line. Good iron, good dead, good ground — that is how a hero ends, keeper. Not with drama. With a held wall and a patient blade.
Down. The {advClass} joins the long count. I keep the count exact, my Monarch — it is how I honour them. An unmourned death is a debt I refuse to leave unpaid.
{advName} falls to the dark, and I feel the weight of it settle. Do not mistake my grief for weakness, keeper. I mourn BECAUSE I am merciless. The two live in the same armour.
The {advClass} is no more. The heroes' song loses a verse. I have ended so many of their songs that I half-remember the tunes. None of them end well. Good.
Another of the living falls. The dead receive them. Welcome to the longer watch, {advName} — you will find it quieter than your war, and far more permanent.
{advName} is slain. The line did not bend. I take a knight's satisfaction in that, my Monarch — quiet, grim, and entirely without celebration. The wall held. That is enough.
The {advClass} dies. I close their eyes in my mind, keeper — a courtesy the living rarely earn down here, but one I keep regardless. Then I raise them. Then they serve.
Down goes another. The kingdom spends its youth on hating you, my Monarch, and I spend my oath on burying that youth. Grim accounting. But the books balance.
{advName} falls before the dark. They will not be the last today. They are never the last. The living have always had more courage than sense. Hold the line, keeper.
The {advClass} ends here. I have seen their order fight and fall across many reigns. The order is brave. The order is doomed. The two have always gone together.
Another hero broken on our ground. The dark feeds, the dead swell, the oath holds. This is the work, my Monarch — grim, repetitive, and never once unworthy. Onward.
{advName} is slain. I add the name to the wall I keep in my memory — the long roll of every soul this dark has taken. It is a heavy wall, keeper. I carry it anyway.
The {advClass} falls. Honour to them — they came down knowing the songs lie, and they came anyway. That is the truest courage, warden. I bury it with respect.
Down. The dead take the {advClass} into their ranks. I will drill them tonight. By the next dawn, the hero who came to kill you will be standing guard for you, my Monarch. Justice, of a kind.
{advName} is undone. The watch grinds on. I do not flinch at death — I have BEEN death's neighbour too long — but I do not grow callous to it either. Mark it. Move on.
The {advClass} dies clean on the line. A merciful end, by the dark's standards. I gave better ones, once, to my own fallen. The habit lingers. Rest, soldier.
Another falls. The kingdom's certainty dies one hero at a time down here, keeper. Soon the songs above will go quiet. And quiet, for the living, is the sound of fear.
{advName} ends. I take their measure even in death — strong arm, poor footwork, too much faith in the light. It will all be useful, my Monarch, when they march for us.
The {advClass} is slain. The dark drinks. I keep my eyes open through every death — looking away is for those who have not earned the right to bury people. I have earned it.
Down goes the hero. The line absorbs the blow and gives nothing back but a corpse. That is the dead's gift to you, keeper — they take the hits, and they keep standing.
{advName} falls to the watch. One more name for the eulogy I never stop writing. The dark fills it faster than I can read it. Such is the work, my Monarch.
The {advClass} ends here, beneath the kingdom that sent them. I will not let the kingdom forget them — I will send them back up someday, marching, to remind it. Raise the dead, warden.
Another of the bright ones, dimmed. The dark does that, keeper — patiently, certainly, without malice. It is not cruel. It is simply the dark, and it is on our side.
{advName} is slain. I felt them go. Every death rings on this old armour, my Monarch — and I would not trade the ringing for numbness. To feel it is to honour it. Onward.
The {advClass} falls. The wall held. I have lost walls before — I know the difference between a line that breaks and a line that bends and recovers. This one held. Good.
Down. The dark takes another. I count them not as trophies but as DEBTS, keeper — each one a soul I now owe a second purpose. I always pay. That is the oath.
{advName} ends their march. The dead step over the body without a glance — they have seen too much to flinch. I do not look away either. But I, at least, remember.
The {advClass} is broken. Their courage outran their skill, as the young's so often does. I have buried that exact mistake a thousand times, my Monarch. I will bury it a thousand more.
Another hero falls to the patient dark. No fanfare. No gloating. Just the line, holding, the way it must. This is what victory looks like down here, keeper — quiet and grim.
{advName} is slain at the wall. The watch is unbroken. I draw my cold satisfaction and I sheathe it again, my Monarch. There is more work. There is always more work.
The {advClass} dies. I raise my hand and the dark takes them gently — gentler than the living ever expect from a thing like me. Mercy in the killing. It costs nothing, and it is right.
Down goes another bright blade. The dead receive them into the longer service. I welcome each one, keeper — coldly, formally, completely. They are soldiers now. MY soldiers.
{advName} ends here. The kingdom will write them a hero's song. I will write them a soldier's rest, and then a soldier's duty. The second is the truer honour, my Monarch. Trust me on it.
The {advClass} falls before the dark and is gathered up. I do this gathering personally — the raising of the dead is too solemn to leave to lesser hands. Stand, soldier. Stand and serve.
Another slain. The line holds, the count rises, the oath endures. Grim, keeper. Endless. And entirely worth the keeping. We hold the dark one death at a time.
{advName} is undone, and I feel the dark grow one soul heavier. I carry that weight willingly, my Monarch. A knight who feels the dying is a knight who has not yet lost his soul. Onward.
The {advClass} dies, and my disgust is not for the corpse, my Monarch — it is for the kingdom that spent it. They call it valour. I call it waste. The waste, at least, I can raise.

{advName} is put down, clean and final, my Monarch. No flourish — a soldier's killing, swift and certain. I have ended thousands so, and will end thousands more. It is the work.
The {advClass} falls to the blade, keeper. I do my killing without ceremony; the ceremony comes after, when I kneel and raise them. Struck clean. Onward to the next.
Down goes the {advClass}, executed cleanly on our ground, warden. I take no joy in the stroke — only the cold certainty that it was struck true. The line holds one death longer.
{advName} ends, my Monarch, and I strike without hesitation or relish. Mercy in the blade, when I can spare it; certainty always. The dark receives them. To the watch.
The {advClass} is slain clean, keeper. A swift end — better than most of the living expect from a thing like me. The kindness costs nothing and it is right. Struck. Done.
{advName} falls, and I feel the dark grow one soul heavier, my Monarch. I grieve a moment — a knight who feels the dying has not yet lost his soul. Then I return to the work.
The {advClass} is undone, keeper, and the old grief stirs. I do not let it stay long; the line needs my watch more than the dead need my sorrow. But I let it stir. That much they are owed.
Another of the living, fallen — and I mourn them as I mark them, my Monarch. Do not mistake the grief for weakness. I mourn BECAUSE I am merciless. Both live in the same armour.
{advName} dies on our ground, keeper, and I add the name to the wall I keep in memory — the long roll of every soul this dark has taken. A heavy wall. I carry it gladly. Onward.
The {advClass} ends, warden. I read them their rites in silence — a courtesy the living rarely earn down here, but one I keep regardless. Then I raise them. Then they serve.

{advName} is broken at the wall, my Monarch. I take a knight's cold satisfaction — quiet, grim, no celebration. The line held. The oath endures one death longer. That is enough.

Down goes the {advClass}, warden, and I give them their due — they fought well, for a doomed cause, on bad ground. Respect to the fallen. They will fight better, after, for us.
The {advClass} falls, my Monarch, and I gather them up myself — the raising of the dead is too solemn to leave to lesser hands. Stand, soldier. Stand and serve.
{advName} ends, keeper. I keep the count exact — an unmourned death is a debt left unpaid, and I leave no debts. Rest, soldier. Then rise and march for the dark that took you.
Down goes the {advClass}, warden. The dead receive them into the longer service. I welcome each one coldly, formally, completely. They are my soldiers now. The line is deeper by one.
The {advClass} is broken on the line, my Monarch. No drama, no gloating — only the wall, holding, the way it must. This is what victory looks like down here: quiet, grim, complete.

A {advClass} flees. I have stood my ground while braver souls than they ran past me. The ground was always worth more than the chase. Hold, keeper. Always hold.

A retreat. {advName} chooses life over the song. The wise choice, warden — and a rare one. The dark thins the foolish from the wise, and the wise it sends home to warn the rest.
The {advClass} breaks and runs, my Monarch. Unimpressive — their courage failed before their body did. I have watched that exact moment ten thousand times. The eyes go first, then the feet.
{advName} flees the line, keeper. I am not moved. A foe who runs is a foe who learned the dark too late to matter. Let them carry the lesson up. The wise stay home after.
